name: Boom Overture (Pre-order Standard)
funFact: A modern standard for upcoming sustainable supersonic travel, engineered to run entirely on sustainable aviation fuels to bypass carbon emission limits.

name: Boeing 737 MAX 8
funFact: Its winglets stand taller than a one-story house and recover enough efficiency to save airlines millions in fuel over a fleet's lifetime.

name: Airbus A220-300
funFact: Passengers love the A220's oversized windows and huge overhead bins, features that make a small jet feel unexpectedly roomy.

name: Airbus A321neo
funFact: The engine fans on this jet are 81 inches wide—significantly wider than the entire cabin diameter of most small private jets.

name: De Havilland Dash 8-400
funFact: The Q400 is a turboprop, but it cruises so fast that it can match jet block times on many short regional routes.

name: Embraer E190-E2
funFact: Nicknamed the 'Profit Hunter,' its oversized engines make it the most fuel-efficient single-aisle aircraft in the regional market.

name: Ford Trimotor 'Tin Goose'
funFact: Produced in 1926 by Henry Ford, its corrugated all-metal design allowed airlines to establish the first dependable transcontinental air passenger routes.

name: Tupolev Tu-144
funFact: The Soviet Union's counterpart to Concorde, breaking ground as the absolute first commercial transport aircraft to break the Mach 2 threshold.

name: Concorde
funFact: The legendary icon of commercial supersonic flight, cruising gracefully above the speed of sound with a mechanical droop-nose assembly optimized for airport visibility.

name: Douglas DC-3
funFact: Arguably the most influential airliner ever built, its legendary durability allowed airlines to operate profitably purely on passenger revenue without government mail subsidies.

name: Boeing 757-200
funFact: Pilots affectionately call it the 'Rocket' because its incredibly massive engine power-to-weight ratio allows it to launch safely out of high-altitude short runways.

name: De Havilland Comet 4
funFact: The world's first commercial jetliner. After early design flaws were corrected, the updated Comet 4 safely inaugurated the jet age across the Atlantic.

name: Boeing 767-300ER
funFact: The absolute reliable standard pioneer of ETOPS safety ratings, allowing efficient twin-jets to replace older three-and-four engine aircraft across transoceanic routes.

name: Airbus A380-800
funFact: The 'Queen of the Skies' is so large that many global airports had to undergo multi-million dollar renovations just to accommodate its wingspan at the gate.

name: Spaceline Skylon (Hypersonic Concept)
funFact: An advanced concept utilizing pre-cooled hybrid air-breathing rocket propulsion to hit sub-orbital hypersonic velocities, making international flights a matter of minutes.

name: Lockheed L-1049 Super Constellation
funFact: Featuring an elegant triple-tail fin layout and dolphin-shaped fuselage design, it represented the absolute golden peak of transoceanic piston-engine air travel.

name: Airbus A300B4
funFact: The historic aircraft that started it all for Airbus, breaking ground as the world's very first twin-engine widebody commercial airliner.

name: Airbus A330-300
funFact: A highly successful medium-to-long haul widebody workhorse, prized by global flag carriers for its incredibly consistent operational economic baseline.

name: Boeing 777-200ER
funFact: The first commercial airliner drafted entirely using computer-aided 3D design software, engineered with the largest diameter turbofan engines in aviation history.

name: Airbus A350-900
funFact: Features ultra-modern carbon-fiber wing profiles modeled on bird aerodynamics that dynamically twist and morph continuously mid-flight to conserve fuel.

name: Lockheed L-1011 TriStar
funFact: Technologically ahead of its time, its highly advanced autopilot system allowed it to land itself entirely hands-off during zero-visibility weather conditions.

name: Boeing 787-9 Dreamliner
funFact: Its composite fuselage lets cabin pressure stay closer to sea level, which is why travelers often step off long Dreamliner flights feeling less worn out.

name: Boeing 727-200 Advanced
funFact: Featuring a unique T-tail layout and three rear-mounted engines, its high-lift wings allowed it to serve shorter runways at smaller regional airports.

name: McDonnell Douglas DC-10-30
funFact: A heavy widebody tri-jet option engineered with its prominent center engine straight-through trunk tunnel mounted inside the vertical stabilizer fin.

name: Ilyushin Il-96-400M
funFact: A widebody quad-engine option built for dense long-haul networks, heavily optimized for robust structural redundancy.

name: Boeing 747-400 'Queen of the Skies'
funFact: The definitive hump-backed cultural legend of international flight that democratized global travel through massive four-engine scale.

name: Airbus A340-600
funFact: An exceptionally long quad-engine design that features a unique additional middle center landing gear bay wheelset to distribute its extreme weight.

name: Boeing 777-9 (777X)
funFact: Features a unique set of motorized folding wingtips that bend upward at the terminal gate so it can fit inside normal airport parking lanes.

name: Boeing 777-300ER
funFact: The undisputed flagship king of point-to-point trunk networks, pairing ultra-reliable twin-engine economics with massive long-range capacity.

name: Boeing 747-8 Intercontinental
funFact: The absolute final passenger evolution of the historic 747 lineage, boasting scalloped engine nacelle chevrons explicitly tailored to curb cabin engine noise.
